title: Machine initialization hangs on windows server
body:
### Issue Description

I have windows server machine on AWS and I am trying to start podman machine there, according to the manual[1]. When I run `podman machine init` it hangs (currently for an hour) without producing any output.


[1] https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/docs/tutorials/podman-for-windows.md

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue
1.  ` podman machine init`
Or:
1. `podman machine init --cpus=2 --memory=2048 --rootful`

### Describe the results you received

the command hangs

### Describe the results you expected

Podman initialises guest virtual machine

### podman info output

```yaml
podman.exe version 4.6.0 (tried for 4.5.0 as well)



> systeminfo

### Podman in a container

No

### Privileged Or Rootless

None

### Upstream Latest Release

Yes

### Additional environment details

```
$ aws ec2 describe-instances --instance-id <>
"Hypervisor": "xen"

                    },

### Additional information

_No response_

comments.body:
Can you use wsl2 on this system?

@n1hility Ideas?

The manual[1] says "If WSL has not been installed on your system, the first machine init command will prompt a dialog to begin an automated install. ", so I didn't try that, actually


[1] https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/docs/tutorials/podman-for-windows.md#automatic-wsl-installation
yes it should install it, but there can be a problem with wsl2 so just trying wsl2 directly should help to see if this is a podman problem or if wsl2 is just not working.

```
> wsl --install
Installing: Virtual Machine Platform
Virtual Machine Platform has been installed.
Installing: Windows Subsystem for Linux
Windows Subsystem for Linux has been installed.
Downloading: Windows Subsystem for Linux
Installing: Windows Subsystem for Linux
Windows Subsystem for Linux has been installed.
The requested operation is successful. Changes will not be effective until the system is rebooted.
```

But with manual install, it seems that I hit https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/wsl/store-release-notes#known-issues.

Does this bug affects podman?
@fedinskiy How are you planning to interact with podman, are you using it from a remote desktop session, or using it in a CI setting, or using ssh?
@n1hility for now I am investigating it via ssh, for future use in CI setting

@fedinskiy ah ok, so in the temporary case you can use an RDP client, which will give you a full session, from there you can use a graphical terminal (e.g. powershell or windows terminal). A little trick you can use to get an ssh prompt from there if you want one, is to run sshd directly in the windows terminal with key-based authentication (password will not work). Since I assume you are running the OpenSSH service already, you would have to bind this on another port (or alternatively  shutdown the service first). To set this up you need to run a terminal as admin and copy the host key out of `C:\ProgramData\ssh`, and then create a .ssh/authorized_keys file, and finally pass the host key to sshd e.g:

```
sshd -d -p 1025 -h $HOME\my-host-key 
```

Note that this will support one session and exit after you disconnect. Also you can't kill your RDP session.

In the case of CI, the easiest workaround to use is Auto Login which will create an active windows session. You can then use a Windows Run entry for that auto-login user to launch your CI agent, which will then have a full usable session. You probably also want to have another entry that locks the screen just as a precaution. Note that If your CI system insists on doing through AWS UserData, that also runs as session 0, so in that case you can use sysinternals psexec  tool to run tasks against the auto login session. This option is a bit more complicated since you need to spool console output. We have a packer config that supports both approaches (Cirrus CI uses UserData) that might be a helpful reference:

https://github.com/containers/automation_images/blob/main/win_images/win-server-wsl.yml
https://github.com/containers/automation_images/blob/main/win_images/auto_logon.ps1

You'd need convert that to json for the packer toolchain.

The example using psexec for the latter case is here:
https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/contrib/cirrus/wsl-env-launch.ps1

Hope that helps!

@fedinskiy oh one other AWS UserData approach I forgot to mention that might be easier. You can use an auto-login setup as described above to start podman machine with a Run registry entry.  You can then ssh normally and interact with that machine using standard podman commands even in session 0 since they are remotely communicating with the instance in the autologon session.
I'm going to close this one since is not an underlying bug, but feel free to reopen if you find something you think is one. Also happy to continue discussion either way.
